flashcards.Back: A configuration on a Cisco router interface (using 'ip address dhcp') that allows the device to automatically obtain its own IP address and subnet mask from a central server.
The process where a router forwards DHCP broadcast messages from a local subnet to a centralized DHCP server on a different subnet, converting them into unicast packets.
The Cisco interface command that enables the DHCP relay agent. it instructs the router to forward UDP broadcasts for multiple protocols, including DHCP, to a specific server.
The set of protocols forwarded by default when a helper address is set, including DHCP (67/68), DNS (53), Time (37), TACACS (49), TFTP (69), and NetBIOS (137/138).
When a relay agent receives this broadcast message, it changes the source to its own interface IP and the destination to the DHCP server's unicast address.
A field in the DHCP packet header that the relay agent populates with its own interface IP address. The DHCP server uses this to identify the correct pool for the client.
The verification command used to confirm if a helper address is correctly configured on a specific router interface.
The specific Cisco IOS command entered in interface configuration mode to make a router act as a DHCP client on that port.
A vendor-specific DHCP option used by Cisco lightweight access points to discover the IP address of their Wireless LAN Controller (WLC).
A Cisco-proprietary DHCP option that provides a list of TFTP server IP addresses to VoIP phones for downloading configuration files.
An industry-standard DHCP option used to provide the IP address or hostname of a TFTP server to a client.
A DHCP option used to specify the boot file name that a client should download from a TFTP server (used in PXE booting).
Also known as Option 82. it allows a relay agent to insert additional information about the client's physical location (like the switch port) into the DHCP request.
A network design where a single high-availability server handles IP addressing for all subnets, rather than running individual DHCP processes on every router.
The message sent by a DHCP server back to the relay agent. The agent then converts it back into a broadcast or unicast to the original client.
The destination port used for DHCP messages sent from a client or relay agent to a DHCP server.
The destination port used for DHCP messages sent from a DHCP server to a client.
A security feature that prevents rogue DHCP servers from operating on a network by only allowing DHCP responses from 'trusted' ports.
A message sent by a client to the DHCP server to inform it that it no longer needs the assigned IP address, allowing the server to return it to the pool.
A unicast message sent by a client to its original DHCP server after 50% of the lease time has passed to extend the current lease.
A broadcast message sent by a client if it cannot reach its original server to renew a lease (at 87.5% of lease time), attempting to extend the lease with any server.
Negative Acknowledgment. A message from the server to the client indicating that the requested IP address is not available or is invalid for the subnet.
A message from the client to the server indicating that the offered IP address is already in use on the local network (detected via ARP).
A message sent by a client that already has a static IP but needs additional configuration parameters like DNS servers or domain name.
A permanent mapping of a specific MAC address to a specific IP address in the DHCP server, ensuring the client always gets the same IP.
The set of contiguous IP addresses defined in a DHCP pool that the server is allowed to dynamically assign to clients.
The DHCP parameter that provides the client with its default gateway address for the subnet.
The DHCP parameter that informs the client of the local DNS suffix (e.g., cisco.com) to use for name resolution.
A configuration where a router has two or more 'ip helper-address' commands on one interface to provide redundancy for the DHCP service.
A Cisco DHCP pool command used to specify the IP address of the next server in the boot process (usually the TFTP server).
